ideas.Existence precedes essence:
"Existence precedes essence" is the founding formula of Sartre's **existentialism** — the compact slogan in which he sums up his entire philosophy of human freedom. To grasp it, consider a manufactured object, say a paper-knife. Its **essence** — its definition, purpose, and properties — exists in the artisan's mind *before* the knife is made; the object is produced to fulfill a predetermined function. Here essence precedes existence. For most of the Western tradition, and above all where God is conceived as a creator, the human being was understood the same way: God, like a supreme artisan, creates humans according to a fixed conception, a "human nature."

Sartre, an atheist, denies this. There is no God, and therefore no pre-given human essence, no blueprint, no fixed nature. For human beings, the order is reversed: **existence precedes essence**. "Man first of all exists, encounters himself, surges up in the world — and defines himself afterwards." We simply *are*, thrown into existence, and only then, through our choices and actions, do we make ourselves into what we are. There is no human nature to appeal to, because there is no God to conceive it.

The consequences are momentous. If we have no given essence, then we are **radically free** and **wholly responsible** — we are "condemned to be free," the sole authors of our values and our selves, with no excuses and nothing to fall back on. Man "is nothing else but what he makes of himself." This is the source both of the exhilaration and the anguish of the existentialist condition, and the root of Sartre's ethics of authenticity and commitment.
ideas.Radical freedom and bad faith:
**Radical freedom** and **bad faith** (*mauvaise foi*) are twin pillars of Sartre's philosophy, developed in *Being and Nothingness*. Because existence precedes essence and there is no fixed human nature, Sartre holds that human consciousness (which he calls the *for-itself*, *pour-soi*) is fundamentally **free** — indeed, that we are "condemned to be free." We cannot *not* choose; even refusing to choose is a choice. There is no situation, however constrained, in which we do not confer meaning on our circumstances and choose our response to them. This freedom is total and inescapable, and with it comes total **responsibility**: we are the uncontested authors of our lives and values.

This absolute freedom is vertiginous, and it produces **anguish** (*angoisse*) — the dizziness we feel before our own limitless responsibility, with nothing to guide or excuse us. To flee this anguish, human beings constantly resort to **bad faith**: a species of self-deception in which we lie to ourselves, disowning our freedom by pretending we are *not* free. In bad faith, we treat ourselves as fixed *things* determined by our roles, our nature, our past, or our circumstances — as if we were objects (the *in-itself*, *en-soi*) rather than free consciousnesses.

Sartre's famous example is the **waiter** who plays at being a waiter too eagerly, sinking his freedom into a role as if he were nothing but a waiter, a thing with a fixed essence. To live in bad faith is to deny one's own freedom and evade responsibility; to live in **authenticity** is to acknowledge and own one's radical freedom. This analysis is among the most penetrating accounts of self-deception in modern philosophy.
ideas.The look of the Other:
"The **look** (*le regard*) **of the Other**" is Sartre's celebrated analysis, in *Being and Nothingness*, of how our relations with other people fundamentally shape our self-consciousness — and why, on his account, those relations are marked by conflict. Sartre asks how we come to know that other minds exist and how they affect our own being. His answer centers on the experience of being *looked at*.

Consider his famous example: I am peering through a keyhole, wholly absorbed, a pure consciousness spying — and suddenly I hear footsteps; someone is watching me. In that instant everything changes. Under the Other's gaze I am no longer a free, transcending subject; I feel myself *objectified*, turned into a **thing** with fixed properties — "a peeping Tom," "a jealous man" — pinned down and defined from outside. The Other's look reveals to me that I have an "**outside**," a being-for-others that I do not control, and I experience **shame**: the recognition of myself as an object in someone else's world.

This gives Sartre his solution to the problem of other minds — the Other is not inferred but *experienced* directly, through the look that seizes and objectifies me. But it also makes human relations essentially a **struggle** over freedom. In looking at me, the Other makes me an object; in looking back, I try to reduce the Other to an object and reclaim my subjectivity. We cannot both be free subjects at once. This is why Sartre concludes, in a line from his play *No Exit*, that "hell is other people" — the perpetual, unwinnable contest of gazes for the position of subject.
ideas.Engaged literature:
"Engaged literature" (*littérature engagée*) is Sartre's influential theory of **committed writing** — the doctrine that literature and, more broadly, the intellectual's work must be socially and politically engaged, taking responsibility for the world. Developed above all in his essay *What Is Literature?* (1947) and through the journal *Les Temps modernes*, it follows directly from his existentialist ethics of freedom and responsibility. If human beings are radically free and wholly responsible for the world they make, then the writer cannot claim a position of pure aesthetic detachment; to write is already to act, and to remain silent is itself a choice with consequences.

Sartre argues that **prose** in particular is inherently a form of action. Words are "loaded pistols"; the prose writer, by naming and disclosing the world, reveals situations of injustice and thereby calls readers to their freedom and to the demand for change. "The writer has chosen to reveal the world and particularly to reveal man to other men so that the latter may assume full responsibility before the object which has been thus laid bare." To name an injustice is already to change it, because it can no longer be looked at innocently. The engaged writer addresses the freedom of readers, appealing to them to take up the causes of their age.

Sartre distinguished the freer situation of poetry, music, and painting, but held prose fiction and the essay to a standard of commitment. He embodied the ideal himself as the archetypal "committed intellectual," intervening in the great political struggles of his day. The theory shaped decades of debate about the responsibilities of art, though it was later challenged by writers who defended literature's autonomy.

works.Being and Nothingness:
*Being and Nothingness* (*L'Être et le néant*, 1943) is Sartre's philosophical masterwork and the systematic foundation of his **existentialism** — a vast, dense treatise subtitled "An Essay on Phenomenological Ontology." Written during the German occupation of France, it synthesizes the phenomenology of Husserl and the ontology of Heidegger into a powerful original vision of consciousness, freedom, and human existence.

At its core is a fundamental distinction between two modes of being. The **in-itself** (*en-soi*) is the being of things — solid, complete, self-identical, simply *what it is*. The **for-itself** (*pour-soi*) is the being of consciousness — which is precisely *not* a thing, but a "nothingness," a perpetual openness and lack that can never coincide with itself. Consciousness introduces **negation** and possibility into the world; it is always beyond itself, free, unfixed. From this ontology flow Sartre's famous themes: **radical freedom** ("we are condemned to be free"), the **anguish** of total responsibility, and **bad faith** (*mauvaise foi*), the self-deception by which we flee our freedom and pretend to be fixed things.

The book also contains Sartre's brilliant phenomenology of concrete existence — the analysis of the **look of the Other** and the conflict at the heart of human relations, the body, temporality, and desire. It culminates in the bleak conclusion that human beings are driven by a futile longing to become a self-grounded fusion of for-itself and in-itself — to become, in effect, God — a project doomed to fail, so that "man is a useless passion." Difficult and exhilarating, *Being and Nothingness* is one of the central works of twentieth-century philosophy.
works.Existentialism is a Humanism:
*Existentialism Is a Humanism* (*L'existentialisme est un humanisme*, 1946) is Sartre's most famous and accessible work — the published version of a wildly popular public lecture delivered in Paris in 1945, as existentialism became a cultural sensation. Intended as a *defense* of his philosophy against its critics (both Communists, who called it bourgeois and quietist, and Christians, who called it despairing and amoral), it distills the central themes of *Being and Nothingness* into clear, vivid, and quotable form. Sartre himself later had reservations about its simplifications, but it remains the single best entry point to his thought.

Here Sartre articulates his signature formula — "**existence precedes essence**" — and its consequences: since there is no God and no fixed human nature, human beings are **radically free** and wholly responsible for what they make of themselves. He explains the accompanying moods of **anguish**, **abandonment** (facing a world without God-given values), and **despair**, and insists that in choosing for ourselves we implicitly choose for all humanity, choosing an image of what a human being ought to be.

The lecture is famous for its concrete example of a young man torn between caring for his mother and joining the Free French Resistance — a dilemma no rule or theory can resolve, illustrating that in the end one must simply *choose* and thereby invent one's values. Against the charge of pessimism, Sartre argues that existentialism is in fact a **humanism** and a doctrine of action, optimism, and dignity, because it places human destiny squarely in human hands. Enormously influential, it defined existentialism for a generation.
works.Critique of Dialectical Reason:
The *Critique of Dialectical Reason* (*Critique de la raison dialectique*, 1960) is Sartre's massive, difficult, and ambitious later work — his attempt to reconcile the existentialism of his youth with **Marxism**, which he had come to regard as "the unsurpassable philosophy of our time." Where *Being and Nothingness* had centered on the free individual consciousness, the *Critique* wrestles with history, society, collectives, and the material conditions that constrain freedom — seeking to give Marxism the theory of individual human praxis and lived experience that Sartre thought it lacked.

Sartre's goal is to ground the intelligibility of history in individual human action, or **praxis** — purposive activity in a world of scarcity. His key and pessimistic concept is the "**practico-inert**": the way our own actions congeal into structures, institutions, and material forces that then turn against us, dominating and alienating their makers. Human freedom is thus perpetually caught and deflected by the inert weight of what it has itself produced. Against this, Sartre analyzes how scattered, serialized individuals can fuse into a genuine collective agent — the "**group-in-fusion**," born in moments of revolutionary action — and how such groups then tend to ossify back into institutions and constraint.

Sartre thereby tries to trace, from the standpoint of individual praxis, how collective realities like classes, institutions, and history itself become intelligible without dissolving human freedom into impersonal economic laws. Notoriously dense and left unfinished, the *Critique* was central to debates in Western Marxism and existential social theory, even as the structuralism of the 1960s was rising to challenge Sartre's humanist emphasis on the free subject.
